data.title: Apple: Next CEO Should Rent Out Siri, Not Rebuild It
data.description:
I am revisiting my bull case on Apple Inc. (AAPL) after the announcement that John Ternus will become CEO on September 1, 2026, with Tim Cook moving to executive chairman.

In my view, this CEO transition comes at a moment when the market is still debating what Apple should be. The key question that John Ternus has to address is whether Apple is an AI platform, a hardware innovator, or a cash compounder built on distribution and ecosystem control.

My core view remains constructive, although it's conditional on one factor. Apple must stay disciplined on AI. By disciplined, I mean reducing as much as possible any cash burn (some call it investment) in any in-house LLM models or in the new upgraded Siri.

As proven by Q1 results and Q2 guidance, consumers aren't that concerned with AI features as investors are. Therefore, the fact that the company is behind the Mag 7 in the AI race is actually a positive. Why?

Simply because Apple owns the distribution channel, particularly in the U.S. In my view, charging LLM providers to solve Siri queries makes significantly more sense than building data centers and developing their own LLM model to do the same thing.

I'll be clear. If the new CEO pivots toward a costly and lower return effort to compete directly on foundation models, the bull case becomes materially less attractive. Until I see signs of this pivot, I remain optimistic about the company, and I reiterate my strong buy rating.

Tim Cook Stepping Down as CEO

The main headline this week is the announcement that John Ternus will become CEO on September 1, 2026, while Tim Cook becomes executive chairman.

Ternus is 50, has a 25 year history inside Apple, and is widely recognized as a product focused engineer. According to Apple, he joined the product design team in 2001, became a hardware engineering vice president in 2013, and joined the executive team in 2021.

So, he’s a relatively new executive and product perfectionist, which is both a plus and a minus.

On the positive side, Apple has been perceived by the market as a laggard in the AI race. You may remember the March 2025 announcement that these Siri upgrades (i.e., the new Siri) were delayed to 2026.

Well, we're well into 2026, and the old Siri is still there. Now, does that really matter? It turns out the answer is no, as I discussed in a previous coverage. Here is an excerpt from the last earnings call that proves that end users don't care as much about AI as investors do:

And based on that, we're in a supply chase mode to meet the very high levels of customer demand. We are currently constrained. And at this point, it's difficult to predict when supply and demand will balance.

On the negative side, as a product perfectionist, I am afraid he may double down on the AI strategy, or even worse, on the Apple Vision Pro.

I have to say that I'm not encouraged by Apple’s $3,499 Vision Pro headset, especially when Meta’s Ray Ban smart glasses had become a surprise hit at a fraction of the Vision Pro's price. That's why the wearables segment growth is pretty much nonexistent.

As for the AI strategy, I argued many times that Apple's edge is distribution, not intelligence. That's why I was so encouraged by the Gemini partnership earlier this year. I certainly prefer Siri to run on Gemini (or route prompts to this LLM) rather than Apple creating its own LLM model.

In fact, headlines like the one below are particularly encouraging.

Why?

In my view, the more AI providers like Gemini or Claude compete for placement, the higher the bidding price to be the default LLM on Siri. Similar to search. To be clear, Apple hasn't announced a paid default-placement auction for Siri yet. In my view, this is a potential catalyst worth monitoring.

Therefore, I would keep a very close eye on the new CEO's remarks in September, as a U-turn toward Apple Intelligence (run by an in-house LLM model) would be detrimental to the stock, in my view.

Why build your own model and burn tokens when you can charge others to be the default LLM provider? That’s why I like Tim’s current strategy.

More Leadership Changes

Aside from the main CEO headline, Arthur Levinson will shift from non-executive chairman to lead independent director, and Ternus will join the board.

A quick look at the latest 14A shows the stock holdings among the top beneficial owners. Tim Cook is the 4th largest owner of shares, and Arthur Levinson is the 3rd.

Therefore, I wouldn't be surprised to see a wave of insider selling activity prior to the transition.

Speaking of insider activity, it would be very encouraging to see John Ternus opening longs on the company after (or even before) he takes over Tim in September.

The transition plan also includes promoting Johny Srouji to chief hardware officer, effective immediately, filling the most obvious hole created by Ternus’s promotion.

There are a lot of moving parts, so I will keep a close eye on the next earnings call for any hints that suggest a U-turn on the AI distribution model of the company. Speaking of earnings, here is what I'm monitoring heading into the Q2 2026 earnings release.

Q2 Preview

Apple guided Q2 2026 revenue growth to 13% to 16%. The consensus was looking for only 10%, so management's guidance prompted upward revisions to the Street's revenue models:

The stock price is still lagging behind the 12-month revenue forecast trend (blue line below), so that's a good sign.

What I want to see in the next earnings call is continued momentum in iPhone and Services, which reached new all-time highs back in Q1 2026. Interestingly, back in Q1, Apple led global smartphone shipments and grew 5% yoy even as overall industry shipments fell because of memory shortages.

Therefore, I think analysts will double down during the Q&A on how higher memory prices affect demand and the company’s bottom line. I say double down, because in the last call, one of the analysts asked the following:

Maybe to start with -- there's a lot of focus on the impact of memory to a host of companies, and I'd love to kind of get your perspective when you folks are guiding gross margins up into the March quarter. Just talk about, A, your comfort in securing the bits that you need for shipments? And B, how do we think about memory inflation flowing through Apple's model over time?

Here is Tim's reply:

From a memory point of view, to answer your question, memory had a minimal impact on the Q1, so the December quarter gross margin. We do expect it to be a bit more of an impact to the Q2 gross margin and that was comprehended in the outlook of 48% to 49% that Kevan gave earlier.

Beyond Q2, we don't obviously provide outlooks beyond the current quarter. But we do continue to see market pricing for memory increasing significantly. As always, we'll look at a range of options to deal with that. So hopefully, that gives you the full view.

I’m not sure about you, but that still didn’t clear up the main question investors had: whether Apple would raise prices or absorb the cost pressure as memory pricing goes up.

For reference, I include below TrendForce's latest forecast for memory prices in Q2 2026 :

Overall, if I see a slow Q3 FY26 gross margin guidance, that could be an indication that the company is absorbing costs. On the contrary, if I see a slow Q3FY26 revenue growth guidance, that may suggest that they are passing the costs to consumers.

For reference, the Street is looking for 8.75% yoy growth in Q3 FY26. In fact, the trend below is not encouraging:

Valuation

Despite not being in the AI race, the stock is trading at a relatively rich premium. Investors are paying today 32x for next year's earnings. In comparison, I include a table showing the Magnificent 7 group:

Apple isn’t cheap, although, in my view, 32x forward earnings is not in overvalued territory. I also think there’s room for multiple expansion if the company surprises the Street’s top-line models, as it did in Q1 FY26.

Looking Ahead

Heading into the September CEO transition, I would keep a very close eye on Ternus’s messaging on AI.

As I noted in this piece, my bull case holds as long as Apple keeps treating AI as a distribution layer and monetizes default placement on Siri, as the Gemini partnership suggests.

I would get really spooked if the new leadership takes a U-turn and follows the steps of Meta in its AI strategy.

In the upcoming earnings release, I’ll closely monitor how much memory inflation shows up in Q3 FY26 guidance, both in revenue and gross margin, after Q2’s 13% to 16% revenue outlook and 48% to 49% gross margin guidance. Even if management doesn’t disclose whether it plans price hikes or will absorb the jump in memory costs, the guidance will likely be a telltale sign.

Overall, I remain bullish on Apple. My conviction didn't change much, however, I sold my position earlier this year, as I've been raising cash during the Hormuz selloff. A few weeks back, I decided to bet on the AI bottlenecks, as I believe there is more juice to be squeezed (i.e., higher returns).

data.title: Ban On Corporate Home Buying? No Sweat For American Homes 4 Rent
data.description:
There are very few bipartisan issues when it comes to our national government.

Priorities can be diverse, and the focus of each political party, or even each political candidate, can heavily vary based on the electorate that they're trying to appeal to. One aspect of everyday life that we can all agree on is that over the last decade, the cost of goods has risen heavily.

Typically, inflation is a quiet force that slowly erodes the value of each dollar you have in your savings account. When it moves at the desired 2%-3% rate, you don't really notice it from day to day or even from year to year. It's the slow drip that erodes value, yet if you think about it over multiple decades, the cost of things has risen quite a lot.

It wasn't that long ago that there were folks complaining to me about how gas used to be able to be purchased for 50 cents a gallon. It was seen as unimaginable that gasoline would break above the $2 a gallon point or even the $3 a gallon point. You're getting the same amount of gasoline, but the value of your dollar buying it becomes less and less. This is the impact of inflation.

A major catalyst for inflation in the last decade was the massive government bailouts and spending by governments around the globe to restart their economies after COVID-19. Instead of it taking three decades for inflation to become heavily impactful, we experienced a massive spike in inflation in 10 years. Even though inflation may be slowing down as a whole, it is a cumulative effect. Inflation, when measured, is the rate at which goods are getting more expensive, or your dollar is getting less valuable. That means when inflation slows, all of that previously built-up devaluation is still there.

This leads us back to what has become a bipartisan issue within our national government, and that is the focus on. Affordability. How to achieve affordability may not be agreed upon, but the need for affordability in the average American life has become a forefront issue. One aspect of the American dream that is getting a lot of attention regarding affordability is housing costs. The cost to buy a house has risen dramatically because, typically, home values climb in line with inflation. So with the rapid increase in inflation since COVID-19, home values have rapidly climbed as well, but individuals earning power has not. Your annual paycheck increase is meant to help you keep pace with inflation, but when inflation was spiking to high levels, it was not offsetting that effectively. Therefore, the prices of homes have climbed with inflation and faster than real wage-earning power.

So what is the solution? For some, they believe that it's not inflation's fault (the government's fault) that home values have climbed so rapidly, but that it is big corporations' fault. They believe that if corporations stopped buying homes, there would be a sudden decline in demand for them, and their values would fall quickly. This is a double-edged sword when taken at face value. You might be able to achieve a lower price for those individuals who haven't purchased a home yet and are willing to do so. You also risk hurting all of those who are using their home's equity as a retirement tool or a line of credit. If all of their homes suddenly get devalued, you're now hurting all of those individuals as well. You're helping some to hurt by hurting others.

Interestingly, homes owned by corporations are not a massive part of the overall housing market. Brookings determined that large institutional investors own only 3% of the single-family rental market. This reality hasn't stopped all levels of the American government from considering measures to try to increase affordability by reducing market activity by corporations or large institutional investors. You're considered a large institutional investor if you own 100 or more homes. The government loves to blame someone else for issues that they've caused, and corporations are an easy target.

So how can you and I benefit from this wide-scale misunderstanding and the likelihood that there really will not be anything effectively done by the government to solve the problem?

Today, I want to examine an opportunity that has sold off due to investors' fears of potential governmental action.

A Solution: Build, Don't Buy

One solution to avoiding any issues of institutional owners buying more homes is to not buy homes but to build them. Today's focus will be on American Homes 4 Rent (AMH). AMH is a single-family rental-focused real estate investment trust (REIT).

In January, President Trump signed an executive order aimed at reducing institutional investors' perceived bidding up of the housing market. He additionally signed further executive orders attempting to make housing more affordable in March. Overwhelmingly, however, the impact of these executive orders has been muted on the housing market, as home values continue to be strong even in the face of higher interest rates slowing down house-buying activity.

AMH was founded in 2012 and has focused on owning and renting single-family dwellings. Furthermore, they've moved from being a purchaser of properties to a developer of them, meaning that overwhelmingly they are now building homes that they then turn around and rent out or building communities that they rent out the homes within.

Moreover, AMH regularly sells homes into the housing market, meaning that they are now a net provider of homes, not a purchaser and consolidator of home ownership.

This allows us to sidestep many of the issues highlighted by the government when it comes to institutional ownership of homes. Even if they were to ban the ability for institutions or large institutions to purchase homes, it would not prevent them from building them. It would be silly to think that the government would actively prevent homes from being built while trying to increase housing affordability and overall housing stock.

AMH is structured as an REIT, meaning that the vast majority of its revenues must be doled out to shareholders as dividends. They can only retain about 10% of their taxable revenue at most to maintain their REIT status, and therefore, shares must be issued, or debt must be used to continue growth. Another option is capital recycling. This is one way AMH effectively funds its ability to continue to reward shareholders and grow. They will build homes, which they then turn around and sell at a profit, distributing what they must to shareholders and recycling the capital into additional home development. As long as home prices remain elevated and home demand remains strong, they're able to do this quite profitably.

Financial Check Up

AMH has a wide footprint across the United States. This allows them to adapt to the varied economic conditions of each region.

This adaptability is highlighted in the strength of their tenant base. Having wonderful properties in multiple markets is meaningless if you're unable to have a strong tenant base that's paying you the rent that you need them to pay or that's expected.

AMH's overall tenant base is quite strong, and they seem to have few issues when it comes to rent being paid on time or effectively. This is also highlighted by their stable renter base, with their overall rented properties remaining above 90%.

AMH maintains a strong balance sheet and enjoys a BBB credit rating from S&P Global with a stable outlook. AMH has no major debt maturities until 2028. Plenty of time to refinance or handle the debt maturity effectively.

When it comes to top-line and bottom-line growth, AMH has effectively grown for multiple years.

This means AMH is not seeking to grow at any price, nor is it lax in its focus. Overall, per-share funds from operations (FFO) growth of 12% and top-line growth of 19% reflect their ability to grow without diluting shareholders heavily.

Shareholder Returns

As an REIT, AMH's primary form of shareholder returns will come from its distribution. This is because a REIT, as a registered investment company (RIC), must pay out 90% of its taxable revenue to shareholders to maintain its REIT status. Looking over the last decade, AMH has posted some very strong returns as a whole.

It has readily outperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index ETF (VNQ) and, until recently, largely kept up with the general market as measured by the State Street SPDR 500 ETF (SPY). I don't expect REITs to match the overall SPY total return. This is because they're very different in makeup from most of the growth stocks that are in the overall market. However, the fact that it's readily beating the VNQ is a major positive. The share price performance is wonderful, but as a dividend-focused total return investor, I'm also looking for strong dividend reliability and growth. AMH also offers a large positive in this category as well.

In February, AMH hiked its quarterly dividend by 10% to $0.33 a share. This marks the 5th year of AMH growing its dividend and generates a compounded annual growth rate over those 5 years of 37.53%. Combined with its current 4.51% yield, we get a massive Chowder Number over 41%. I do not expect AMH to keep growing its dividend at this pace, but high single-digit to low double-digit growth is possible so long as FFO continues to grow as it has.

Value Proposition

Currently, AMH trades well below its historical 10-year price-to-AFFO of 25.54x (blue line). I do not expect that this price-to-AFFO ratio will be restored, largely because it's heavily impacted by the extreme overvaluation in 2020 and 2021.

We've decided to use a more conservative rate of 21x price-to-AFFO as our forward valuation (purple line). This is actually the low that AMH has historically hit before bouncing back higher again, and we feel that it is an extremely conservative measure for forecasting. We would rather forecast conservatively and enjoy additional upside than forecast aggressively and see underwhelming results.

Looking forward, this gives us a total return expectation of 31.9% by the end of 2027 and annual returns of 10% through 2031.

AMH is cheaply valued currently, well beyond its usual rebound point, due to negative sentiment and fears concerning government intervention, which, as we examined, doesn't directly impact AMH in reality.

Risks

There are a few risks to consider when it comes to AMH.

The first is governmental risk. The government's intervention in the housing sector has not been large or overwhelming at this time because there has not been a bipartisan agreement on what to do, just that there is something that needs to be done. The federal government can issue executive orders, but that does not create an immediate market reaction as far as home builders and home transactions are concerned. They can place additional red tape to make things more difficult for corporations, but they cannot force a change directly. An act of Congress would be required to stop corporations or large institutional investors from buying single-family homes, and the likelihood of that being a long-term solution is very low.

The second risk to consider is the location risk of various AMH properties. Some are exposed to wildfire risks on the West Coast or to hurricane risks when you look at Southern states. These can drive additional repair costs if there is damage to multiple properties unexpectedly for AMH, as they are the landlord and must repair the home for the renters. I rate these risks to be on the lower end of overall concern, but they are ones to recognize that if there is a large wildfire or a massive hurricane that hits where their properties are located, they will have immediate negative impacts on their earnings.

Conclusion

With a deeply attractive valuation and long-term total return outlook tied together with a highly attractive overall dividend growth profile and current dividend yield, AMH is an extremely compelling investment.

Market sentiment, especially when it comes to concerns about what the government is going to do in the future, can drive prices down heavily in the short term, but in the long term, fundamentals will win out, providing us with an attractive overall return profile. This is exactly the kind of investment that we are always looking for to add to our Dividend Kings Model Portfolio.

Take a step beyond the political noise, the news cycle, and the chaos of daily life and step into the clarity of data-driven fundamental analysis. Here you will find comfort, reality checks, and a portfolio that will provide for your financial needs. Live like royalty.

data.title: CGDV: Differentiated Strategy Likely To Yield Superior Results For Long-Term Holders
data.description:
Introduction

Ever since Capital Group's Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) popped up on my radar months ago, I was instantly drawn to their differentiated investment strategy.

While there are ETFs with significantly longer track records, CGDV's investment approach makes me believe they're likely to yield superior results vs. peers.

If you're someone looking for growth and income, well, I believe CGDV gives you the perfect blend of both, making it ideal as a long-term core holding.

In this article, I discuss their recent performance, differentiated strategy, and why they're likely to yield superior results vs. other ETF peers alike.

Initial Coverage

In my initial coverage, The Dividend Fund That Doesn't Behave Like One: CGDV's Market-Beating Strategy, I rated CGDV a buy.

Their compelling blend of growth, income, and outperformance since inception led me to believe they were ideal for a core holding.

While their track record was still relatively short, their unique approach, sector allocation, and concentrated portfolio led me to believe they could continue delivering strong returns.

Since CGDV is in the green, up over 5%, while the S&P 500 Index (SP500) is up closer to 4% in the past 4 months.

CGDV's Performance YTD

CGDV gets a lot of love here on Seeking Alpha, likely due to its different investment approach and performance since going public on February 22, 2022. But some investors may remain cautious as the ETF has less than 5 years of performance.

For me, I usually like to see at least 5 years' worth of performance, but this is not a hard stop for investment consideration. Strategy is most important. And again, CGDV's differentiated approach is what makes them stand out among peers.

You can see in the chart below that CGDV has still performed well in comparison to peers. The Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) leads the pack, up close to 12% year-to-date.

Some may mention how CGDV is overrated and SCHD, which has a much longer and proven track record, is the superior fund. They may also mention how CGDV's actively managed approach doesn't give them an advantage vs. SCHD, which is passively managed.

While I like SCHD and own it in my Roth, investors should look into why SCHD has outperformed CGDV YTD. I believe this has to do with the SCHD's sector allocation.

Call it perfect timing, but SCHD's higher exposure to the Energy (XLE) sector and reconstitution towards dividend growers in March helped its performance. The conflict in the Middle East resulted in a spike in oil companies.

As a result, SCHD trimmed Energy and shifted towards Technology (XLK) and Healthcare (XLV). CGDV's higher exposure to the Technology sector weighed down its performance.

Although Technology is in the green now, the sector went through a brief correction starting in February. That's why you can see from the chart above how CGDV and Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV) saw the sharpest declines of 5.0% and 4.8%, respectively.

Since December, CGDV's top 10 hasn't changed much except for them swapping out Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) in its top 10 for Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT), likely due to Starbucks' persistent headwinds.

Currently, FDVV's tech exposure is 24% compared to 29.5% for CGDV. SCHD's is less than half that at 11.2% after their recent reconstitution. But long-term, I believe CGDV has a higher chance of outperforming SCHD.

And the reason is their higher tech exposure. With the rapid growth of AI likely to continue, I foresee strong returns for CGDV going forward. So, in my opinion, the lower growth CGDV has seen so far in 2026 should not be a deciding factor on them being lower quality.

Multi-Manager Approach Is An Advantage

Instead of repositioning the portfolio annually like many peers, CGDV's multiple portfolio managers decide when to reposition the portfolio. Most ETFs use an algorithm, but CGDV's managers dispose of companies whenever they see fit.

Below are their 5 portfolio managers, each specializing in specific sectors. All the current managers have been with the fund since 2022, except Brittain Ezzes, who came on in 2025. I believe this gives them an advantage to deliver superior results. This is similar to the saying “two heads are better than one.”

Below is a chart showing CGDV's total returns vs. peers. CGDV delivered returns of 29.19% in its first full year. Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO) was second, up roughly 27%. SCHD lagged, up less than 5%.

In 2024, the fund still delivered solid total returns close to 21%. But it was beaten by its peer group, except for SCHD.

And last year, they again outperformed the group, up close to 26%, with VOO in second, up 18.12%.

CGDV was actually designed to outperform the S&P, so them outperforming VOO in 2 of the 3 years shows their strategy is working. And they expect to do this by investing in larger, established dividend-paying companies.

Most of CGDV's concentrated portfolio is in the U.S., but they do invest a small portion internationally with 2.3% exposure to Canada, 3.1% exposure to EMEA, and 2.0% exposure to China.

This also makes them appealing long-term, as it provides diversification outside the United States and allows them to capture some growth from international companies.

And lastly, their higher tech exposure should benefit them, as I believe large tech companies' AI CapEx spending is likely to create a multi-year growth runway.

AI has already been disrupting the job market as companies have been cutting jobs. And this will likely continue as companies continue to adopt AI to cut costs.

A recent survey stated 48% of companies are planning layoffs this year. Technology giant Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) announced they plan to cut 10% of its global staff starting next month, likely due to AI.

Qualified Distributions

Capital Group's Dividend Value ETF's name can be misleading. This is because their dividend yield is much lower compared to their peers. This is why I mentioned in my previous article that it behaves like a growth fund.

Currently, CGDV's dividend yield is 1.25%. But what they lack in yield, they make up for in growth. Since inception, distributions have grown over time from $0.03 to their latest $0.11 paid on April 1st, 2026. Lastly, their distributions are qualified. This means you can hold them in a taxable account or an IRA.

What Could Go Wrong

Although I believe CGDV's multi-manager strategy will yield superior results, this is no guarantee. In 2024, they were outperformed by both VOO & FDVV, both passively managed funds.

Moreover, CGDV's approach hasn't been tested in steep, prolonged market corrections, as they're less than 5 years old. For some investors, this may be a turn-off, and I genuinely understand.

When seeking companies to invest in, I like to see how an individual company or ETF performed, particularly during difficult times like the Great Financial Crisis or the pandemic of 2020. So, this is something for investors to keep in mind with CGDV.

Furthermore, we could see muted market returns for 2026, particularly if the conflict in the Middle East fails to get resolved. Although the market appears optimistic, further negotiation failures or military action against Iran could lead to underperformance.

My Expectations & Conclusion

Going forward, unless we see a steep market crash or correction due to a recession, I believe CGDV can deliver minimum total returns between 15% and 20% going forward.

The reason is their higher exposure to Technology, multi-manager approach, portfolio concentration, and exposure to international companies as well.

I also like their double-digit exposure to the Healthcare and Industrial sectors, which I believe gives them some downside protection should we see a prolonged bear market.

If you're looking for a core holding that gives you growth and income, then the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF may be attractive to hold as a long-term, core holding.

data.title: Apple: Tim Cook Stepping Down On A High Note (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Tim Cook's tenure at Apple Inc. (AAPL) is coming to an end, with the 15-year CEO announcing plans to step down in September.

While Cook's time at Apple cannot be described as anything less than extraordinary, some investors grew increasingly frustrated with Apple's lack of innovation in the later years, decelerating growth, and decision to watch the AI game from the sidelines.

Ahead of an upcoming Q2 earnings report that should be very strong, the question is whether Tim Cook is exiting on top or whether new CEO John Ternus has a plan to reignite Apple's growth trajectory.

Tim Cook's Tenure Comes To A Close

When Tim Cook took the helm at Apple, its market cap was $350 billion, it was generating $108 billion in annual revenues, and its profit stood at $26 billion.

Fast forward fifteen years, and Apple has more than quadrupled its sales and profits, and its market cap has risen more than 10x, surpassing a $4 trillion valuation. Under his leadership, Apple was at many times the world's most valuable company. In short, I think it's fair to say Tim Cook has done quite a decent job.

That said, the last few years weren't short of criticism.

Increasing Frustration With Apple's Conservatism

It's funny, as an analyst covering companies like Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), I consistently see investors frustrated with overspending, over-aggression, and a constant desire to run after the next big thing.

Meanwhile, at Apple, criticism is exactly the opposite. If there's one word that defines Tim Cook to many, it's that he was a world-class operator and optimizer. Apple has built supply chains that are as efficient as can be, pushing the envelope of imagination. Similarly, its fixed costs and operational leverage were exceptional.

In the process, though, it appears that Apple may have turned the nod too much towards profits and conservatism and too little towards growth.

Apple decided to shut down its car project, which could have been a huge growth story, highly anticipated by Apple fans. And more importantly, it decided to effectively stay out of the AI race, a decision that's been highly debated.

The bulls argue that Apple is capturing value from AI growth through its App Store fees and will leverage third parties' hard work to make its devices AI-pilled. For example, the revamped Siri will be based on Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Gemini, a model that costs tens of billions of dollars to train and that has a dedicated, very expensive lab working every day to improve it.

That said, Apple is clearly capturing minuscule value in this game relative to others, and the risks of becoming dependent on third parties (something Tim Cook always preached against), as well as being potentially disrupted by an AI-native device company in the future, are very real.

While I am excited about the Mac Neo prospects, the fact that it's the company's main growth story as Cook is stepping down is a letdown.

What To Expect In Upcoming Earnings

With the help of easy iPhone comps, FX tailwinds, and a strong iPhone 17 lineup, Apple delivered a five-year high-growth rate in the December quarter.

iPhone sales grew 23%, and Services rose 14%, led by China, up 38%; Asia Pacific, up 18%; and the Americas and Europe, both up low double digits.

Margins were also very strong, with record gross margins for both products and services, driving a record operating margin of 35.4% in the quarter.

Looking ahead to Q2, Apple expects to deliver another mid-teens growth quarter, guiding for revenue growth in the range of 13%-16%, again led by iPhone and Services. Mac could see higher growth rates as well, driven by easier comps, although the impact of the Mac Neo will be more of a Q3 story, as it was launched in March.

Apple also guided for continued improvements in margins across the board, showing the memory crunch is more of a supply issue than it is a cost issue for the company at the moment.

Generally, at times of supply constraints, Apple typically guides very accurately, and I think there's a very high chance of upside surprise in the April 30th print.

To me, the two most significant factors to monitor in earnings will be commentary about Tim Cook's decision and guidance for Q3, which should bake in Mac Neo, memory prices, and much tougher iPhone comps. The next quarter, and not this one, is going to really tell the story about Apple's near-term prospects.

Valuation

At $273 per share, Apple trades at 32 times forward earnings and 29 times next year's estimates.

This is 10% higher than Apple's five-year average and puts Apple as the highest valued stock in big tech, especially when accounting for growth.

Apple is expected to grow earnings and revenues at the slowest clip among the group, and no, it's not just about scale; Amazon is 60% larger based on sales, and Google isn't too far behind either.

Apple's valuation reflects the simple fact that the market views Apple as having the widest moat in big tech. And I don't think the market is wrong.

Building a hardware and physical supply chain has been proven to be much harder than competing at the software level, and nobody comes close to Apple on that front, with much of the credit belonging to Tim Cook.

My issue with Apple's valuation is that it already reflects said moat. Then, the question is, where will future market-beating performance come from?

If Apple's stock maintains its current multiple and rises alongside earnings, we're looking at 10%-11% annual returns. Quite decent, but far from amazing. Add to that the risk of at least some multiple contraction, and you get to average market returns.

Bottom line, for me to get excited about Apple as an investment, either the valuation needs to come down or the growth story needs to improve.

Enter John Ternus, Apple's New CEO

Apple announced that Tim Cook will become executive chairman of Apple’s board of directors and John Ternus, senior vice president of Hardware Engineering, will become Apple’s next chief executive officer effective on September 1, 2026. The transition, which was approved unanimously by the Board of Directors, follows a thoughtful, long-term succession planning process.

John Ternus worked under Steve Jobs and considers Tim Cook as his mentor. He's been at Apple since 2001, starting on the product design team. In 2013, he became VP of Hardware Engineering, and in 2021, he was elevated to the executive team as Senior VP of Hardware Engineering.

Apple said Ternus was instrumental for multiple product lines, including the iPad, AirPods, and multiple generations of iPhone, Mac, and Apple Watch. He also led the MacBook Neo effort.

Overall, it seems to me that Ternus is very much a 'Hardware Guy,' and I'm not really sure how much of a change he's going to be as CEO. Perhaps his strategic views are different from Cook's, but that is something we'll only be able to learn over time.

In addition, some challenges Apple is currently facing, around tariffs, geopolitical tensions, and rising memory prices, are right in the wheelhouse of Tim Cook, and it remains to be seen what will be Ternus' way of dealing with those issues.

Conclusion

Tim Cook's tenure at Apple will be remembered as one of the legendary founder-succession stories in history.

He's leaving Apple on a high note, and the new CEO has his work cut out for him, needing to supercharge the company's growth prospects, starting at a much higher valuation, and facing important questions regarding the company's AI plans, as well as multiple operational challenges.

Ternus has a very impressive record, but on paper, he doesn't seem to be a major change from Cook. Time will tell if that's a good or a bad thing.

Ahead of earnings, I upgrade Apple to 'Hold,' as valuation reflects a wide moat and a near-term growth spurt but bakes in downside risk long-term.

data.title: Join The 1% By Earning +8%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

When we discuss the 1%, most people picture private jets, yachts, and a lavish lifestyle. There is a general sense of aspiration to lead that kind of lifestyle, travel around, and swim in abundant wealth. But few people aspire to do the necessary things to achieve success that the self-made billionaires did.

Very few… achieved their success primarily through sheer luck, by swinging for the fences with stocks, or betting on horse racing or lottery tickets. The majority did so through hard work and perseverance and by being deliberate with their capital, selective with their opportunities, and disciplined in how they invest. They aren’t winning by reacting faster, trading more, or staring at charts all day. In fact, they’ve intentionally stepped away from that noise.

They don’t micromanage every position, they don’t panic when prices dip, and they don’t rebuild their portfolios every time the market throws out a tantrum. They operate differently.

Furthermore, they construct portfolios designed to function without constant supervision, engaging experts where appropriate, while maintaining deep diversification, allowing cash flows to do the heavy lifting. Most importantly, many design strategies and plans for the long term and run businesses that are designed to maintain profitability across market conditions. Billionaire and Amazon founder Jeff Bezos was once congratulated for a strong quarter. He thanked them and noted that the success of that quarter had been “baked in” years earlier and that he is now focused on a quarter 3-4 years in the future.

When volatility hits (it always does), the strategy gets adjusted, not overhauled. Successful investors and business leaders aren’t fearful sellers but opportunistic buyers.

This is how we operate at High Dividend Opportunities. Our portfolio is our business, and it maintains diversified operations. There are segments that do well, while others face headwinds, depending on market conditions. And then there are holdings that quietly make contractual payments (dividends and interest), letting us enjoy the safety while getting rewarded periodically. It’s about owning the right structures, maintaining discipline, and allowing consistent income to compound over time.

Let us now dive into our top picks to help you build a durable, income-rich retirement.

Pick #1: DMLP – Yield 13.5%*

The news these days is all about oil prices, shipping routes, refineries, and pipeline flows. Headlines swing from “war may escalate” to “ceasefire talks,” and oil can jump 10% one day and fall 15% the next. But hydrocarbons remain the lifeblood of any economy. Whether prices are high or low does nothing to diminish their fundamental importance.

Dorchester Minerals, L.P. (DMLP) is a quiet winner from the disruption. As an owner of mineral royalties and net profit interests, DMLP earns from barrels of oil extracted from its acreage, and the amount they earn is determined by the commodity price and the production volume.

For over three years, the U.S. has been the largest producer, so one side of that equation is favorable. The situation in the Middle East has resulted in a sharp increase in spot prices, which creates pathways for higher earnings and larger distributions.

When we bought DMLP in December for our model portfolio, we did so strategically when oil was trading at multi-year lows and when the spare capacity among key oil-producing nations was running thin. The markets were bearish on oil, but our purchase was with a longer-term view that energy prices cannot artificially be held low for very long.

*Note: DMLP is a Master Limited Partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 to investors. It is very important to recognize that DMLP pays variable distributions, and payouts are highly correlated to energy price volatility and production volumes.

Regardless of energy price volatility, DMLP has beaten the S&P 500 in the past ten years. This period includes two major oil crashes and a significant negative outlook for hydrocarbons with the green energy transition, recession fears, and periods of low supply and underinvestment, which led to surging prices.

Unexpected Tailwinds Ahead

In March, the company announced a settlement regarding leaseholds in Midland County, where the partnership owned NPI. This $15.5 million settlement will result in a per-unit boost of $0.32 (based on 48,255,450 common units as of December 31, 2025), positioning Q2 distributions over $1/share. Readers must remember that DMLP has a lag between oil prices and the distributions. Typically, payouts for the current quarter are based on volumes and prices of the previous quarter. In this case, the May payout will be based on Q4 and early Q1 price levels, which had WTI at ~$60/Bbl. Originally, we would have projected $0.60/share, but with the settlement, it will come to ~$0.92/share.

War Premium Will Be Sticky

The lawsuit settlement is obviously a one-time thing, and a positive one for us. Let’s now focus on the macroeconomic events that are positioning us for higher distributions as we look ahead.

Oil prices have been volatile since the war in the Middle East. Oil shot up above $100/Bbl for several days in March and April. We can expect a higher distribution level in Q3, which will be difficult to project precisely.

Consideration A: We will assume the current volume run-rate of ~11,750 BOE/D; every $10 move in WTI (from $60) will add ~$0.12–$0.15/Bbl to the quarterly distribution. So WTI above $100 could result in Q3 distributions above $1.10/share.

Consideration B: Due to the extent of the disruptions and destruction of energy infrastructure in the Middle East, we don’t expect any form of ceasefire to bring oil prices back to pre-war levels. A reasonable estimate will be WTI between $75 and $80/bbl, which could result in quarterly distributions at $0.90-0.95/share. But that isn’t the case yet. As I am writing this, oil is hovering above the ~$90 range, which will result in payouts closer to what we calculated in Consideration A.

Looking ahead, a highly conservative $0.95/share quarterly payout over the medium term calculates to a +13.5% annualized yield.

Pick #2: BUSE Preferred—Yield 8%

With 158 years of operating history, First Busey Corporation (BUSE) is the 97th largest bank in the United States, with $18.1 billion in total assets. As of December 31, 2025, BUSE had 79 banking centers, with the majority (58 of them) in the Chicago suburban area, Central Illinois, and the St. Louis Metropolitan Area. Source.

Earlier in 2025, the bank completed the acquisition of CrossFirst Bank, and by the end of the year, management reported that the integration was completed. At the end of 2025, BUSE reported a tangible book value of $20.23/share, a 13% YoY increase. The regional bank maintains regulatory capital levels exceeding regulatory minimums, with CET1 at 12.2% and a leverage ratio of 11.3%. These represent strong capital buffers, giving plenty of capacity to absorb losses and remain financially stable in the event of a crisis.

BUSE’s loan book shows a conventional commercial bank model, focused on relationship lending rather than the direct-lending/private credit strategies that have been in the headlines recently.

Moreover, BUSE’s focus is on relationship banking, with a very small credit-card-focused business, and most of the regional bank’s debt investments are focused on commercial lending, CRE lending, treasury management, and wealth management. As such, proposals like a 10% cap on credit card interest rates or slashing interchange fees will not affect their operational results.

BUSE has delivered growing dividends and total returns to shareholders over the past ten years, with its $1/share annual payment for 2025 enjoying a comfortable 39.5% payout ratio.

In January, the regional bank announced a 4% YoY increase to the payment of $0.26/share, which calculates to a respectable 4.2% annualized yield. We expected continued adjusted EPS growth to support a growing common stock dividend.

  • 8.25% Series B, Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock, First Busey Corporation 8.25 DEP PF SR B (BUSEP) – Yield 8%.

BUSEP is the bank’s only publicly traded preferred security, rated investment-grade BBB by Morningstar DBRS. The preferred only began trading in May 2025, and the regional bank spent $9.3 million towards these commitments since issuance, in comparison to $81.1 million spent on common stock dividends.

BUSEP has experienced a meaningful pullback following the broader sell-off in the banking sector. Despite trading at a modest premium to par, this perpetual preferred currently offers an 8% QDI yield for at least ~50 months.

Conclusion

The difference between the 99% and the 1% isn’t access to better headlines; it’s a systematic and strategic approach. While most investors are focused on predicting the next big thing, the successful ones focus on building systems that generate reliable cash flow across market cycles. They understand that volatility is inevitable and use it to their advantage.

At High Dividend Opportunities, this is exactly how we approach investing. We don’t try to predict every swing in the market, and we don’t chase headlines. Instead, we focus on building a diversified income engine that is designed to generate reliable cash flow across a wide range of economic conditions.

Some of our investments benefit directly from real economic activity, where stronger demand and higher pricing power translate into higher income. Others provide steady, contractual payments backed by well-capitalized institutions. Together, they create a portfolio where income continues to flow—regardless of short-term market noise.

Because in the end, it’s not about timing the market. It’s about building a portfolio that works consistently, predictably, and in your favor.

data.title: Marvell: Google Fever Is Making Investors Forget The Weak XPU Guidance
data.description:
I reiterate my hold rating on Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL), as the stock is up mid-single digits after The Information reported over the weekend that Google may be in talks with the company about developing two new chips. It appears that one of them would be a memory processing unit designed to work alongside Google’s TPU.

If confirmed, it could be great news for the XPU business, especially after the disappointing FY27 growth guidance from the December update (which, to my disappointment, wasn't meaningfully upgraded in the last quarter).

I'm definitely not buying the stock at this overbought level. That said, I am not bearish on the company either. I simply believe there will be a better entry point once the frenzy calms down. In my view, retesting $100 over the next few weeks wouldn't be far-fetched.

Overall, even if the Google rumor is confirmed by the company, the sources are suggesting that the completion of the design of the memory processing unit would only happen next year. In other words, the upside to FY27 earnings may be more limited than what the market thinks.

The Google News

Essentially, there are two main players in the custom AI silicon space: Broadcom and Marvell.

Broadcom is the de facto leader.

Unfortunately, the company does not break out pure custom-silicon revenue separately from AI networking. In fact, neither of the two does, even though Marvell said at its June 2025 Custom AI Investor Event that custom was more than 25% of data center revenue in Q4 FY25 and expected to become more than 50% over time.

That said, Broadcom's signed multiyear customer commitments are clear proof of how far the company is in the ASICs race. In the March 2025 update, Hock Tan said that Broadcom had three existing hyperscale custom chip customers and four more deeply engaged customers that were not even included in the 2027 revenue opportunity ($60 billion to $90 billion).

Broadcom's names in custom silicon include OpenAI, with a plan to roll out 10 gigawatts of custom chips through 2029; Meta, with an expanded partnership to develop several generations of custom AI chips through 2029; and, most recently, Google.

Well, it turns out that there are some reports out there that suggest that Google may be in talks with Marvell to develop two AI chips, including one tied to the TPU infrastructure.

If confirmed, it could be great news for the custom XPU business. So far, the performance of this segment has been particularly weak. Back in the December update, management expected the custom chip business to grow at least 20% in the next fiscal year.

In the March update, the guidance was “upgraded” from “at least 20%” to “more than 20%.” The wording matters, although I want to see a concrete figure before I can consider this a meaningful upgrade.

Overall, > 20% XPU growth is not the hypergrowth that I want to see.

In fact, if we go back to August last year, some analysts noted that it may take 12-18 months before the custom ASIC business becomes diversified enough to deliver more consistent beats and raises. That comment came in after Marvell’s October-quarter outlook (released in August 2025) implied a decline in custom ASIC revenue. Back then, management said that lumpiness was normal when large cloud providers built out infrastructure. Given the $635B in FY26 CapEx among the 4 hyperscalers, I don't have a high conviction in management's remarks back then.

If the Google rumors are true, I would not expect the upside to materialize this year, especially since the sources suggest the memory processing unit design may not be finalized until next year.

A (Fast) Rerating

As I'm writing, Marvell stock is up mid-single digits in the premarket session of April 20.

In fact, since March 30, the stock is up over 50%, which shows how quickly reratings can happen in the current bull run.

Yes, I said bull run. In my view, the Hormuz sell-off is a bear trap.

Back to Marvell, in December last year, I rated Marvell with a strong buy in an article titled:

Back then, I argued that Broadcom was stealing the show in the custom AI space, arguing that the bull case mainly revolves around the optics segment.

In fact, back in March, I downgraded my rating to a hold after the soft FY27 guidance for silicon/XPU growth:

I'm downgrading Marvell to a Hold as its custom silicon/XPU growth (20% yoy guided for FY27) looks too modest versus stronger AI leaders like Broadcom.

The Google rumor is exactly the catalyst that the stock needs to shift the pessimism around its ASICs business. Why?

Because, so far, all the market has are multicolor slides on the opportunity and the CAGR of the custom XPU market.

I think investors want to see proof in the form of strong guidance, significantly above the Street's expectations. More often than not, beats and raises are often followed by upward revisions in the Street's revenue models.

As you may know, the share price tends to follow the next 12-month consensus (blue line below), so the stock needs more quarters like Q4 FY26.

So far, I am not particularly excited about the forward multiple of the company. As I'm writing, Marvell shares are trading at 36.5x forward earnings. In comparison, Broadcom (the leader in custom ASICs) is trading at 35.6x forward earnings.

I am not sure why paying the same price for next year's earnings would make any sense, given the worst forward revenue and EBITDA growth:

Looking Ahead

From a technical perspective, I like the chart.

I see a clear breakout beyond the $100 and the $130 resistance levels.

That said, I believe shares are stretched and are likely for a pullback. That's why I wouldn't rush in and buy right now after the Google rumor frenzy.

Overall, I prefer to wait on the sidelines until I see a meaningful upgrade in the custom silicon/XPU growth guidance for this year. So far, the 20% yoy guided for FY27 is anything but exciting. That's why I remain on the sidelines.

data.title: GOF: Back To Business
data.description:
Change has been a recurring theme at REITer’s Digest. Over the past weeks, we have talked about changes within various industries, including real estate and, more recently, private credit. The changes have been big and small. In the case of private credit, the industry appears to be going through its great shake-up. However, change can come in many shapes and sizes.

In another discussion of change, we recently talked about how short-term treasuries might just be an attractive opportunity once again as the world becomes more uncertain.

Today, we will talk about how change is not always permanent and sometimes the road can lead you right back where you started from.

This article will follow up on my prior coverage of the Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF). My last coverage of the fund identifies valuation as a key risk factor. I believe this is no longer the case.

Review of Prior Coverage

Around two years ago, I published an article on GOF. The purpose of the article was to introduce one of the market's best-performing closed-end funds. GOF is a unique multi-asset fund from Guggenheim with a flexible investment mandate that allows one of the most capable investment teams to invest on your behalf.

In my prior coverage of the fund, I provided an overview of the portfolio, expense structure, and the fund's conservative use of leverage. Also included in the article was a discussion of recent changes within the fund, including a shift towards high-yield credit, moving away from equities to capitalize on interest rate movement.

In the coverage, there were things that I liked about the fund.

GOF is a flexible fund, designed to adapt alongside the markets. When rates are too low, the fund can target opportunities in the equity markets. Alternatively, over the past two years, GOF has reoriented towards high-yielding credit opportunities. Like all closed end funds, GOF is actively managed.

In almost two years, not much within the portfolio has changed. The multi-asset fund has continued to make a shift towards high-yield credit.

Today, bank loans account for nearly 40% of the fund assets, followed by high-yield credit at nearly 30%. Also in the mix are asset-backed securities and investment-grade corporate bonds. Today, leverage is still around 12% of the funds holdings.

There was a lot to like about GOF including the manager, fund, structure, and historical performance! Up through publication, the fund had even outperformed the S&P 500 since before the financial crisis.

However, I didn’t think everything was sunshine and rainbows.

So What Was The Problem?

At the end of the day, my prior coverage focused on problems with the valuation of the fund. You didn't have to read far to get that impression with a title like The Ascending Valuation Is A Problem. The key piece of my prior coverage was discussing valuation risk within closed-end funds.

Currently, shares of GOF are trading at a steep premium to net asset value. While NAV has remained flat over the past year, GOF’s share price has appreciated as investors chase the fund’s monthly distribution. Shares of GOF are currently trading at a 32% premium to NAV.

As charted above, the current premium is nearly the highest of the past decade. For most of the past ten years, GOF has traded much closer to net asset value establishing a precedent to return to this valuation. GOF’s share price is currently $15.82 per share while net asset value is just $12.06 per share. This leaves significant room for GOF shares to fall, should the valuation begin to compress.

My belief was that GOF’s share price relative to net asset value was simply too high at a 32% premium in October 2024.

Certain funds trade at more persistent discounts and premiums than others, but at the end of the day there is a gravitational pull that often brings investments back towards net asset value. For example, PIMCO funds tend to see wild deviations as high as 50%. But at the end of the day, these funds usually come back near book value.

This only makes sense, given net asset value is the underlying value of the assets. In the case of GOF, moving away from net asset value when the underlying holdings are very liquid is an increasing risk.

Right Back Where We Started From

When I published my prior coverage on GOF, shares were trading at a premium to NAV of 32%. Since publication, shares have declined by around 28%, and total return has been negative, despite high-yield credit, equities, and bank loans performing well over the same period.

Even GOF’s performance on net asset value has been pretty strong!

So, how has performance for the shareholder been negative while NAV return has been so strong? The explanation lies mostly in changes in share price relative to net asset value. Over the past two years, shares of GOF have sold off as they have done in the past.

This leads to a disconnect between price performance and NAV performance.

Zoom back to my original coverage, where GOF was trading at a 32% premium to net asset value, and it appears as though the valuation risk has come to fruition.

Today, shares are once again trading close to net asset value. If we are to look at this relative to the fund’s lifetime, we can see that net asset value has remained relatively steady, while the premium has tended to bound over multiyear periods.

Currently, the fund has once again begun to trade towards a premium after shortly touching a discount to net asset value.

Big Picture

If we are to look at the big picture, we can see that GOF is a good fund. The long-term return on net asset value has been superior to many competing funds and has kept pace with the S&P 500. This means that for periods of volatility, changes in valuation are generally to blame. Over the past 10 years, there have been various opportunities where the fund trades in line with net asset value. Historically, the fund has then returned to a premium to net asset value. The fund has also performed very well against other asset classes over the past decade or so.

For investors who are looking to take advantage of discrepancies in closed-end fund valuations, GOF appears to be one of the most attractive current opportunities. If you are an investor looking for a high-yield multi-asset closed-end fund, GOF is one of the best when you put together all the factors.

My last coverage of the fund identifies valuation as a key risk factor. I believe this is no longer the case. GOF earns an opportunistic Buy.

data.title: Silicon Motion: Further Upside Beckons As Demand For Enterprise-Grade SSDs Steps Up
data.description:
Background

Back in October 2023, I had initiated coverage on Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (SIMO), which enjoys leadership credentials in the development of NAND flash memory controllers for SSDs [solid-state drives]. My bullish call on SIMO, which was still a “small-cap” fabless semiconductor entity back then, was centred around a potential bottoming out of the NAND flash memory market, a likely pickup in gross margins & collection efficiency, attractive valuations, and favorable risk-reward on the charts.

We've since seen SIMO transition from small-cap status ($1.8B) to mid-cap status (current market cap of sub-$5B), while generating stellar returns of 184%, more than double the returns of the Nasdaq, and 1.57x that of other semiconductor stocks (on average).

Nonetheless, given that it's been roughly 30 months since my last coverage of SIMO, and this entity is also a little over a week from announcing its Q1-26 earnings, I felt an update was long overdue.

Increased Appetite For Enterprise-Grade SSDs

One of the chief reasons why I am still optimistic about SIMO despite its sturdy run is that I feel the company's controllers are well poised to benefit from the demand for enterprise-grade SSDs used in data centers and hyperscale data centers. Of particular relevance is SIMO's thrust in the high-growth QLC (quad-level cell technology) NAND space, which is expected to be the fastest-growing segment of the broad NAND flash memory controller market over the next 8 years (expected CAGR of 14% through 2034) due to its efficacies in hyperscale storage applications. Even SIMO management is on record stating that the QLC-based storage SSD represents the largest addressable market for MonTitan (the latter is their SM8366 Gen 5 Enterprise SSD Controller, which leverages QLC NAND).

Compared to traditional SSDs, QLC technology can facilitate storage of more data (for the same level of space), driving higher layer counts and lower overall costs per bit, resulting in better hyperscaler and AI infrastructure economics. Meanwhile, the ongoing supply shortage in enterprise HDD [hard disk drive] has also provided another fillip for QLC storage solutions.

Last month, SIMO also came out with its new PCIe Gen5 Enterprise SSD Controller, SM8008, which is designed to also address opportunities in enterprise-grade “boot drive” storage (the likes of Exascend and ATP have already started integrating the SM8008 into their next-gen enterprise SSD platforms). The meaningful spike in AI and cloud infrastructure has no doubt highlighted the importance of AI accelerators and high-performance primary storage, but one shouldn't also play down the utilitarian role of highly efficient (up to 14 GB/s throughput sequentially) and secure boot SSDs like SM8008 that can help drive down power consumption (active power consumption of sub 5W), while also scoring highly on the security front (in compliance with CNSA 2.0 that goes live in 207).

Upcoming Q1 Earnings—Notable Observations

As implied earlier, SIMO's prospects could potentially be enhanced or dampened in a little over a week, as it is due to announce its Q1 earnings. To get more specific, the earnings are due to be released post-market hours on the 28th of April, followed by an earnings call the following day, before the market opens. What are some of the key metrics that investors should be mindful of?

The first thing to consider is that Q1 has traditionally been SIMO's seasonally weakest quarter from a revenue angle, and even H1, in general, tends to be weaker than H2 (as customer orders for semiconductor solutions for mobile and computing devices largely tend to pick up from Q3, ahead of the buildup for the holiday season in Q4).

However, what's interesting to note is that this time around, SIMO management is guiding to a 5-10% QoQ improvement from Q4, and consensus expectations for Q1, both on the topline and the bottom line, have been ramped up quite substantially since SIMO last announced earnings. We've basically seen +30% upgrades on both the EPS and sales over the past 3 months, with consensus now budgeting for a sales figure within the ballpark of $300M and an adjusted EPS of $1.3. Also consider that if SIMO ends up meeting that $300M sales figure, it would represent the best YoY topline performance since Q4-21 (or 17 quarters), when it grew at 84% YoY!

Then, the last time we covered SIMO, we had suggested that the company's gross margins were set to improve, and we've seen this play out over multiple quarters, with the current sub-50% margins above the long-term average of 47%. While this comeback has been encouraging to note, investors should also consider that an unfavorable product mix (higher SSD solutions over controller sales) in Q1 will likely push the Q1 margins back to the range of the long-term average. However, what's telling is that this will only be transitory, as margins in the following quarters are poised to ramp back up to 48-50% as the mix improves through the year in favor of PCIe 5 controllers and their enterprise SSD solutions. Crucially, further down the line, SIMO's operating margins, which have averaged 15% for the last two fiscal years, are expected to expand to 16-18% in Q1!

Given that SIMO pays flattish quarterly dividends of $0.5 per share (they haven't been raised since FY21 and were stalled briefly in FY23 because of a potential acquisition), and the cash on its books has largely been trending lower every quarter since FY24 (although given that SIMO doesn't carry any financial debt, this is not a pressing concern), some attention will likely go towards whether SIMO can generate meaningful positive operating cash (this wasn't the case in Q4, where they only managed $1.5M of positive operating cash, well below the aggregate CAPEX and dividend outlay of less than $25M) as it attempts to cover its dividends and its CAPEX commitments.

I would be concerned by this scenario if the operating cash flow position was being hampered by weak profitability, but that certainly isn't the case here, with net profits more than doubling annually (and growing by 21% QoQ).

The main reason for the operating cash drag is that SIMO has been building up inventories (up by 110% YoY and 25% QoQ), and it's fair to imagine that they wouldn't be resorting to this tactic if they didn't have an inkling of future demand for their products from their key clients (SIMO's client pool isn't hugely dispersed, with the top 5 alone accounting for more than two-thirds of the entire revenue mix). And given that their revenue in Q1 is poised to grow sequentially and explode annually, I would expect a good chunk of those inventories to be liquidated, driving an improvement in operating cash flow in Q1.

Valuations—Not As Cheap As Before, But Hardly Prohibitive Either

As is to be expected, given the meaningful expansion in the market cap since my previous coverage, the forward earnings multiple of SIMO is no longer priced at a discount to its long-term average (rather a 25% premium now), but all things considered, I still don't believe it is prohibitive either. Allow me to elaborate on this.

At a forward multiple of nearly 25x, it's also important not to get too myopic and also consider the degree of earnings growth that this business offers at said multiple. Consensus positioning suggests that you could lock in almost 60% forward earnings growth, which then translates to a very compelling price-to-earnings [PEG] ratio of less than 0.5x.

For some additional context, take a look at Marvell Technology, Inc.'s (MRVL), which admittedly is a more well-rounded offering in the enterprise and data center SSD controller space and something that SIMO can aspire to get to over time. SIMO's earnings multiple can still be picked up at a 32% discount over MRVL's corresponding multiple, and if you bring into consideration the degree of earnings growth on offer, the implied PEG of SIMO is not even a third of what it costs to own MRVL (1.48x).

Technicals—Poised for Another Breakout, With Strong Momentum Conditions In Play

Investors ought to pay attention to SIMO's movements on the weekly time frame, where, since the turn of 2025 until February 2026, the stock had been moving higher, but within the boundaries of an ascending channel (marked by the two black lines). Bullish interest was such that SIMO actually broke past the upper boundary of this channel in February but couldn't quite sustain it (first breakouts typically fail, but a retest usually doesn't). We've since seen a pullback to the lower boundary, and if the bears had the upper hand, we could have seen an even more pronounced correction. However, encouragingly, after retesting the lower boundary in late March (a useful entry point for those who missed out on the initial rally), SIMO has once again recommenced a strong uptrend (two strong green-bodied candles over the past couple of weeks) and now looks set to facilitate a second breakout from its channel, even as it attempts to set new highs.

SIMO's bullish conditions can also be further validated by what's seen on the lower time frame (daily charts), where the stock is now trading above all its key moving averages, especially the crucial 200DMA, where there is a strong upside gap of +40% (note also that the 200DMA isn't flat but upward sloping). The presence of this scenario will likely stoke the interest of momentum players in SIMO.

Closing Thoughts

Despite a meaningful appreciation in the share price of SIMO since my last coverage, I am not disinclined to move away from my buy rating on the stock. The company's enterprise-grade SSDs are well-positioned to exploit demand from hyperscalers and data centers, more so given the cost competency of their QLC technology. I also feel that the exceptional forward earnings growth on offer (near 60% YoY) more than helps justify the 25x earnings multiple (an implied PEG of less than 0.5x). Meanwhile, the stock is also in the midst of strong momentum, trading above all its key moving averages, and is set for a potential breakout from its channel. All in all, there are enough reasons to reiterate my long-standing buy on SIMO.

data.title: Sandisk: A High Beta Play For NAND Memory As AI Demand Surges
data.description:
Sandisk Corporation (SNDK), which used to be controlled by Western Digital Corporation, has accumulated a whopping 2,424% return since it was divested in February of 2025. The company’s current Seeking Alpha ratings are all in the green ahead, as it rose another 15% in the last week. But what do they sell? SanDisk produces memory storage, which in recent months has been in high demand for AI hyperscalers. To run large language models, High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) or Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) is required while the model is in use. But this is not the SanDisk pure play. SanDisk sells NAND memory, the inactive ingredient used when the model isn’t in use. NAND stores data that is used to train LLMs, and this specific product is where we are seeing incredible returns. Quarterly revenues have only risen roughly 59% in the same time, while the stock price has returned over 2,000%. What's the reason for the boom? Will these conditions continue? And what should we look out for on the next earnings call? I’ll touch on all of that below, but to summarize, I am holding until April 30th on the next earnings call.

Supply

The story of the NAND boom cycle starts in 2023-2024, when the memory market was oversupplied. Economic theory tells us this played a role in driving prices and margins down. Suppliers would then slow production, so big-name competitors like Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), Kioxia Holdings Corporation (KXIAY), and Sandisk invested their money elsewhere. Now, with a new and substantially lower equilibrium inventory level, the AI boom comes into play. Capex investment in AI data centers caused a rise in demand for NAND memory. Short supply with high demand creates high prices and a high-margin environment. For SanDisk, annual revenues in 2023 were $6 billion relative to today’s TTM of almost $9 billion. There are 2 key factors to look at here:

First, SanDisk has a hidden advantage. They entered into a joint venture with Kioxia Holdings. Instead of building their own manufacturing facility, they both split the cost of production out of one jointly owned facility. They are basically roommates in an expensive house.

Second, margins are propped up by high average selling prices (ASP). As stated in the most recent 10-Q filing, “Net revenue increased 61% in the three months ended January 2, 2026, from the comparable period in the prior year, primarily due to a 36% increase in average selling prices (‘ASP’) per gigabyte and a 22% increase in exabytes sold.” While the joint venture keeps costs low, higher prices go straight to the margin.

The result is that costs of revenues are almost identical comparing 2023 to the present, resulting in a $3 billion boost in gross margin. Low overhead and high prices are every company's dream. Additionally, supply is a de facto oligopoly:

I see a market this concentrated and think there would be some pricing power going on here. But SanDisk is the only pure play, whereas other competitors don’t benefit nearly as much from high prices. So they have less of an incentive to restrict supply to keep prices high. My current assessment is that we will likely see overproduction here soon, bringing prices back down to normal levels. But at the current moment, we still may be seeing increases in the short term. Here is a note from Micron's investor presentation:

So, the key to keep in mind is capex from the broader market moving forward. If we see supply ramping up, we are likely to see another cyclical downturn as prices level.

Inventory

Dissecting inventory turnover, I like to look at the overall cash conversion cycle (CCC). This gives us a better understanding of whether they are recklessly riding a demand boom or if they are displaying operational efficiency. When looking at 2025-2026, we see the CCC has compressed from 150 to 104 days. Shocking, I know… with higher demand comes more cash. But what's good for SanDisk is that a falling DIO with unchanged DPO means SanDisk is turning over inventory faster without shortening its timeline to pay suppliers. These are signs of health.

Days' inventory outstanding is also my key metric moving forward. We talked earlier about prices being a major driver for margins, but demand for cloud services is the other side of the coin. If DIO falls, it means demand is still increasing. But if it rises… the opposite is true. In which case, I would recommend selling.

Demand—The Jevons Paradox

To add to the falling demand concerns, innovations, like TurboQuant, are threatening to make the AI process and memory market more efficient. Running LLMs requiring less memory demand would be unfortunate for memory producers, right? This is a modern-day Jevons Paradox. Just like in 1865, when steam engines became more efficient, demand for coal increased rather than decreased. Greater efficiency actually encouraged wider use. I see the same story here with AI memory demand. Sure, in the short term, we may see reduced storage requirements, but in the long term, demand will drastically expand to meet broader AI applications. When I see headlines of memory compression or efficiency, I think of seeded growth.

From a financial perspective, SanDisk saw a major shift in its product segment sales mix. From Q3 2025 to Q2 2026, Cloud/Datacenter sales grew by 123%. In the same period, sales for Edge/Client and Consumer rose 81% and 59%, respectively. What this tells me is that they have locked in growth hyperscalers (cloud/datacenter), who don’t switch suppliers easily.

From a demand perspective, I see more efficient memory requirements, paired with a strong hyperscaler foothold, to be 2 strong indicators for future sales.

Key SID Drivers

The next earnings call is scheduled for 30 April 2026. I’ve synthesized the key SID drivers that should be in focus.

Supply Discipline and Manufacturing Utilization—High average sales prices have been the tailwind for growth. Increases in industry-wide capex would signal a future supply boom, decreasing ASP, and drastically reducing margins.

Inventory Digestion—Sandisk's cash conversion cycle has fallen by 46 days, which will be a strong benchmark moving forward. A growing inventory (DIO), which currently sits at 121 days, is a clear signal of oversupply, which would have downward pressure on prices and margins.

Sales Mix/Demand—Growth in revenues is driven by cloud/datacenter expansion. Share price performance will be largely dependent on those growth expectations.

Financials

To illustrate the aggressive performance relative to memory-producing competitors, the above chart from Capital IQ includes SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF), and Micron. First, SanDisk is the only pure play in the mix here, achieving the concentrated gains from high prices and demand. Although subtle, you can see a very similar pattern, showing the cyclicality of the returns and the sensitivity to NAND price increases. Being the pure play option of the market, SanDisk has minimal downside protection, and we could see these returns drastically reverse should we enter a low-price environment.

Additionally, from a forward P/E perspective, we do not see a clear premium relative to competitors. Earnings have now been priced into the equation and have converged the market to lower multiples. This could mean we are in the late stages of the memory cycle, which would indicate a peak in earnings is among us. However, it would be unwise to assume this cycle will be like the last. With AI capex investment, there is no clear indication that memory supply will outpace demand anytime soon.

Valuation
